title: Special Relativity: From Spacetime Maps to the Rindler Horizon

scenes.title: Maps of Everything That Happens
scenes.description: Learn to read a spacetime diagram: events are points, worldlines are curves, light rides 45 degrees, and the lightcone is causality made visible.
scenes.markdown:
# Maps of Everything That Happens

A **spacetime diagram** is a map where every dot is an **event** — a specific *place* **and** *time*. We draw **space** across the bottom ($x$) and **time** up the side, but we plot $ct$ rather than $t$ so the two axes share the same units.

## Why $ct$ on the vertical axis?

With $c = 1$ (natural units), one second up the page equals one light-second across it. This choice makes light — the fastest thing there is — travel along tidy **45-degree** lines:

$$ x = \pm\, ct \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{slope } = \pm 1. $$

## Worldlines and slope

An object traces a **worldline** as it moves. Standing still is a *vertical* line (you move through time, not space). Moving at constant velocity $v$ tilts the line, and its slope is

$$ \frac{d(ct)}{dx} = \frac{1}{v} \quad (c=1). $$

The faster you go, the more the line leans toward the 45-degree light line — but a massive object can never quite reach it.

## The lightcone = causal structure

The two light rays through an event carve spacetime into a **lightcone**:

- **Inside the upper cone** ($ct \ge |x|$): the event's **future** — everything it can influence.
- **Inside the lower cone** ($ct \le -|x|$): its **past** — everything that could have influenced it.
- **Outside the cone**: *elsewhere* — unreachable without beating light.

A worldline that stays **steeper than 45 degrees** is **timelike** (a valid massive traveler). One shallower than 45 degrees would need $v > c$ and would break cause-and-effect. **The lightcone is causality made visible** — and it is the foundation for every scene that follows.

*Sources:* [Time dilation (Wikipedia)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_dilation), [Hyperbolic motion (relativity)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyperbolic_motion_(relativity)).
scenes.prompt: This is the opening scene of a relativity lesson; nothing has been established before it. Teach diagram literacy, not formulas. The one mantra: 'up = time (ct), across = space (x), and light splits the difference at 45 degrees.' Color conventions used throughout the lesson: light rays / lightcone / 45-degree lines are orange #ffaa33; worldlines and moving objects are blue #4da6ff; impossible / faster-than-light things are red #ff5566; axes, labels, and neutral guides are #aabbcc. c = 1 in natural units, so slope of a worldline is 1/v and light has slope +/-1. When the student changes the velocity slider v, point out that the worldline tips toward the 45-degree light line but a massive object never reaches it (that would require v = c). Good follow-up questions to suggest: 'What does a horizontal line mean?' (an impossible instantaneous jump across space), 'Why can't a worldline be shallower than 45 degrees?' (it would be faster than light and could scramble cause and effect), and 'What is outside the lightcone?' (the 'elsewhere', causally disconnected from the origin event). Keep it playful and intuitive; the only formula is slope = 1/v.

steps.title: Events and axes
steps.description: Every dot on this map is an event: a place AND a time. Space runs across the bottom, ct up the side. Using ct instead of t makes the units match — one second up equals one light-second across.
steps.prompt: Introduce the vocabulary: an EVENT is a single point (x, ct) — a place and a time together. Space is horizontal, ct is vertical. Point out the three sample events: O at the origin (here-and-now), A to the upper right (later and to the right), B to the upper left (later and to the left). Stress WHY the vertical axis is ct and not just t: with c = 1, it puts space and time in the same units so that light will land at a clean 45 degrees, which we reveal in step 3. Neutral color #aabbcc is used for the axis labels and event dots here so nothing is 'accented' yet.

steps.title: Worldlines and slope
steps.description: An object traces a worldline. Standing still means going straight up. Moving at constant velocity tilts the line: its slope is 1/v, so the faster you go, the more it leans toward 45 degrees. Drag v and watch the traveler's line tip over.
steps.prompt: A WORLDLINE is the whole history of an object drawn as a curve. Contrast the two: the vertical blue line at x = -3 is an object 'at rest' — it moves through time but not space. The tilted line through the origin is a traveler moving at velocity v; its slope is 1/v (with c = 1), so a bigger |v| tips it toward the 45-degree light line. The animated blue dots ride up each line as time t advances, dramatizing that a worldline is traced out over time. Encourage the student to drag the v slider and notice the line leans but never reaches 45 degrees — reaching 45 degrees would mean v = 1 = c, only possible for light. Ask: 'What would slope exactly 1 mean?' (light) and 'Could the line ever be horizontal?' (that would be infinite speed — impossible).

info.content:
### Worldline slope

$v = {{toFixed(v, 2)}}$

slope $= \dfrac{1}{v}$ — vertical at $v = 0$, tilting toward the $45°$ light line as $v \to \pm 1$

The faster you go, the closer the line tilts to the 45-degree light line.

steps.title: Light and the lightcone
steps.description: Light rays are exactly the 45-degree lines. They carve spacetime into a cone: everything the origin can influence lies in the upper cone (its future), everything that could have influenced it lies in the lower cone (its past). Outside is 'elsewhere'.
steps.prompt: Reveal the star of the scene: the two orange 45-degree light rays through the origin, ct = x and ct = -x. Because c = 1 they have slope +/-1 exactly. These rays bound the LIGHTCONE. Shade the future cone (upper, ct >= |x|) and past cone (lower, ct <= -|x|) faintly in orange. Explain the causal meaning: the origin event O can only influence events inside or on the upper cone (its future), and can only be influenced by events inside or on the lower cone (its past). The wedges left and right are 'elsewhere' — causally disconnected. Tie back to the worldlines from step 2: notice they all live INSIDE the cone (steeper than 45 degrees), which is exactly what 'slower than light' means. Suggest: 'Where does the moving worldline sit relative to the cone?' (inside it, always).

steps.title: Predict: which is impossible?
steps.description: Here are three travelers, all leaving the origin. One of them is physically impossible. Which line breaks the rules — and why? Make your guess before you advance.
steps.prompt: This is the PREDICT step of a predict-before-reveal pair — do NOT reveal the answer yet. Three candidate worldlines leave the origin, all drawn in neutral #aabbcc so the student can't tell which is 'wrong' by color: candidate A is steep (slope 2, i.e. v = 0.5, safely timelike), candidate B lies exactly on the 45-degree light line (v = 1), and candidate C is shallow (slope 2/3, i.e. v = 1.5 > 1, faster than light). Ask the student to commit to a guess about which one is physically impossible and, crucially, WHY — before advancing. Do not confirm or deny. If they reason about the lightcone from step 3, that is exactly the intended tool: anything shallower than 45 degrees exits the cone. Resist the urge to give it away; the reveal is the next step.

info.content:
### Predict

Three travelers leave the origin:

- **A** — steep
- **B** — exactly $45^\circ$
- **C** — shallow

**One is impossible.** Which line breaks the rules, and *why*?

*Guess before you advance.*

steps.title: Reveal: causality wins
steps.description: Candidate C is the impostor: shallower than 45 degrees means it covers more space than time — faster than light. It leaves the lightcone. Massive objects always stay steeper than 45 degrees; light rides the edge. The lightcone is causality made visible.
steps.prompt: This is the REVEAL step. Recolor candidate C red #ff5566 to mark it impossible, and keep A blue #4da6ff (a valid timelike traveler, v = 0.5) and B orange #ffaa33 (light itself, v = 1, riding the cone's edge). The key idea: candidate C has slope 2/3 < 1, so its velocity v = 1/slope = 1.5 > c — faster than light. It leaves the lightcone and could reach 'elsewhere' events, which would let effects precede their causes (broken causality). Emphasize the general rule this scene has been building to: massive worldlines are always steeper than 45 degrees (timelike, inside the cone); light rides exactly the 45-degree edge; nothing physical is shallower. The lightcone is causality made visible — and it is the load-bearing idea for the entire rest of the lesson. Suggested follow-up: 'What quantity did we just use to decide?' (slope = 1/v, so slope < 1 means v > 1).

info.content:
### Causality wins

**C is impossible.** Slope $< 1$ means $v = 1/\text{slope} > c$ — it leaves the lightcone and could reach *elsewhere*, scrambling cause and effect.

- **A** massive: always **steeper** than $45^\circ$
- **B** light: rides the **edge** ($v = c$)
- **C** would need $v > c$ — forbidden

The lightcone is **causality made visible**.

scenes.title: The Light Clock
scenes.description: A bouncing photon becomes a clock. Watch a moving clock tick slower — and derive $\gamma = 1/\sqrt{1-v^2}$ from nothing but Pythagoras.
scenes.markdown:
# The Light Clock

This scene is an **ordinary-space animation**, not a spacetime diagram: the horizontal direction $x$ is *position*, and the vertical direction $y$ is *height*. (The scenes on either side of this one are spacetime maps — here we watch things move in real space.)


▶ [Watch the standalone animated proof of time dilation](/renderproof?builtin=physics/time-dilation)
## One bounce, one tick

A **light clock** is the simplest honest clock imaginable: a photon bouncing straight up and down between two mirrors a height $h$ apart. Because light *always* travels at speed $c$, the time between mirror strikes is fixed:

$$T_0 = \frac{h}{c} = h \quad (c = 1).$$

Every strike on a mirror — top *or* bottom — is **one tick**. (The tick counters above each clock count exactly these strikes.)

## The moving clock must take a longer path

Put an identical clock on a train moving right at speed $v$. From the platform, the photon can no longer go straight up: by the time it reaches the top mirror, the mirror has slid forward. The photon must travel a **diagonal, longer path** — yet at the *same* speed $c$. Same speed over a longer distance means **more time**. The moving clock ticks slower.

## Pythagoras on a single tick

Look at a single up-going tick — one mirror-to-mirror flight — lasting coordinate time $\Delta t$. The light travels the hypotenuse of a right triangle:

- vertical leg $= h$ (the mirror gap),
- horizontal leg $= v\,\Delta t$ (how far the clock slid),
- hypotenuse $= c\,\Delta t = \Delta t$ (distance light covers, $c=1$).

Pythagoras gives

$$(\Delta t)^2 = h^2 + (v\,\Delta t)^2 \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta t = \frac{h}{\sqrt{1-v^2}}.$$

Comparing to the rest tick $T_0 = h$:

$$\boxed{\,T = \gamma\,T_0, \qquad \gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-v^2}}\,}$$

The **only** assumption was that $c$ is the same in every frame. At $v = 0.6$, $\gamma = 1.25$; as $v \to c$, $\gamma \to \infty$.

## This is real, not a light-clock quirk

Every clock — springs, atoms, *biology* — slows by the same $\gamma$, because a light clock could sit inside any of them and must agree.

- **Muons.** Cosmic-ray muons are created ~15 km up with a half-life of only $\tau \approx 2.2\,\mu\text{s}$ — enough to travel only ~660 m before decaying. Yet they flood the ground: their $\gamma \approx 5\text{–}10$ stretches their clock so they reach us.
- **GPS.** Satellite clocks are corrected by tens of microseconds per day (special relativity slows them ~7 μs/day; general-relativistic altitude speeds them ~45 μs/day, net ~38 μs/day). Skip the correction and your position drifts kilometers off within a day.

*Sources:* [Time dilation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_dilation).
scenes.prompt: This scene is ORDINARY SPACE (x = horizontal position, y = height), NOT a spacetime diagram like the scenes around it. Make that distinction explicit if the student seems confused. The whole idea is 'same speed c, longer diagonal path, therefore more time.' Color conventions: photons and light paths are #ffaa33, the moving clock body/mirrors are #4da6ff, stationary clock and neutral labels are #aabbcc, the Pythagoras/impossible-warning highlight uses #ff5566. The stationary photon bounces straight up and down; the moving photon zig-zags along a diagonal and — because its time runs at rate sqrt(1-v^2) — visibly ticks slower. In step 3 keep the Pythagoras triangle front and centre: vertical leg h (=2 here), base v*Delta_t, hypotenuse Delta_t. The gamma readout is gamma = 1/sqrt(1-v^2). Good follow-up questions: 'What if v were 0.99c?', 'Does your heartbeat slow too?' (yes), 'Why can't the moving photon just go faster to keep up?' (c is invariant). Reality checks: muons (born ~15 km up, would go only ~660 m without dilation, gamma 5-10 saves them) and GPS (~38 us/day net correction).

steps.title: Meet the light clock
steps.description: A light clock is the simplest clock imaginable: a photon bouncing straight up and down between two mirrors two units apart. One round trip — down and back up — is one tick. Because light always moves at speed $c$, this clock is exquisitely honest.
steps.prompt: Introduce the stationary light clock. Emphasize: the photon goes STRAIGHT up and down (no horizontal motion), the mirrors are a height h = 2 apart, and one full round trip is one tick. T0 = 2h = 4 in these units (c=1). Point out the tick counter incrementing with floor(t/2). Photons and the light path are #ffaa33; the stationary clock frame is neutral #aabbcc. Ask: 'What makes this a trustworthy clock?' (c never changes).

steps.title: Predict: the moving clock
steps.description: Now we build a second, identical clock and set it on a train moving right at speed $v$. Before you set it in motion — will it tick **faster**, **slower**, or **exactly the same** as the one at rest? Commit to a guess, then advance.
steps.prompt: A second, identical clock is introduced at rest near x = -1, not yet moving. Pose the prediction: faster, slower, or same? Do NOT reveal the answer. The v slider now appears (default 0.6) but the moving clock's photon is still bouncing straight up in this step — motion is revealed in the next step. The moving clock body/mirrors are #4da6ff. Encourage the student to commit to a guess and justify it before advancing. Resist telling them the answer.

info.content:
### Predict
The **moving** clock is about to slide right at speed $v = {{toFixed(v,2)}}$.

Will its photon complete a tick **faster**, **slower**, or **exactly the same** as the rest clock?

*Guess before you advance.*

steps.title: Reveal: the diagonal path
steps.description: Watch: the moving clock's photon can't go straight up — the mirrors have slid forward, so it must travel a **diagonal, longer** path. But light's speed is fixed, so a longer path takes **more time**. The moving clock ticks **slower**. The half-tick is a right triangle: vertical leg $h$, base $v\,\Delta t$, hypotenuse $\Delta t$ — same $c$, longer path, more time.
steps.prompt: REVEAL: the moving clock is now sliding right (body x = -1 + v*t) and its photon zig-zags along a diagonal because its clock runs at rate sqrt(1-v^2). It visibly ticks slower than the rest clock — compare the two tick counters. The diagonal path trace (animated_line, #ffaa33) shows the longer hypotenuse. The Pythagoras triangle is the star of this step: vertical leg h=2 (#ff5566 highlight), base v*Delta_t, hypotenuse Delta_t (the light path, #ffaa33). Drive home 'same speed c, longer path, more time.' The invariance of c is the SOLE assumption. Point at the triangle when explaining the algebra. IMPORTANT physics note: on screen, BOTH photons move at the same speed c (1 grid unit per second) — verify by eye. The moving photon covers a LONGER diagonal path per tick at that same speed, which is exactly why its ticks take longer. If asked 'do they travel different distances?': per unit time both cover the same distance (same speed c); per TICK the moving photon's path is longer by gamma. The demo runs as a repeating RACE: the moving clock travels from the start line to the lap-end flag, then the lap restarts and BOTH tick counters reset together. Same journey each lap, and every lap the moving clock banks fewer ticks — the repetition is the point, not a glitch. The lap counter bottom-right shows which lap you're on.

add.text: ⚡ At $v=c$: all the photon's speed is horizontal — none left to climb.
It never reaches the top mirror, so the moving clock freezes: $\gamma=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-1^2}}=\infty$. Time stops.

info.content:
### Slower!
The moving photon rides a **diagonal**. Same speed $c$, longer path $\Rightarrow$ **more time per tick**.

Right triangle for one tick — with $h$ the **mirror gap**, i.e. one rest tick as a distance, $h = c\,T_0$:
$$(\Delta t)^2 = h^2 + (v\,\Delta t)^2 = (c\,T_0)^2 + (v\,\Delta t)^2$$

Solve: $\;\Delta t = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{1 - v^2}}$

steps.title: Where the triangle comes from: the planted wavefront
steps.description: Emit a flash. Its circle stays planted at the emission **event** — it refuses to ride along with the clock. That refusal *is* the postulate; the triangle is just its geometry.
steps.prompt: The origin story of the Pythagorean starting point. A flash is emitted at each clock's bottom mirror at t=0. KEY IDEA: each gold wavefront is a circle of radius ct centered on the emission EVENT, planted in space — it does NOT move with the sliding clock. That is literally the second postulate. The rest clock's mirror meets its circle straight overhead at radius h (t=2). The moving clock's mirror has slid, so it meets the SAME planted circle later and off-axis — at that strike the radius is the hypotenuse ct, the slide is the base vt, the mirror height is the leg h: Pythagoras is intercepted, not assumed. The faint red circle is Galileo's counter-proposal: a wavefront dragged along with the clock, which would strike on the rest schedule (t=2, no dilation). Note the moving photon rides exactly ON its planted wavefront the whole flight. Scrub t slowly through both strikes. Live readouts show strike times 2 vs 2*gamma.

add.text: — planted wavefronts: radius $c\,t$, centered on the emission events
— Galileo's ghost: dragged along with the clock (no dilation)

info.content:
**The postulate, drawn**

$h$ is the mirror gap — one rest tick read as a distance: $h = c\,T_0$ (here $h = 2$, so $T_0 = 2$). Squared, it becomes the $(c\,T_0)^2$ of the sum below.

At $t=0$ each bottom mirror emits a flash. Every wavefront is a circle of radius $c\,t$ **centered on the emission event** — planted in space, indifferent to the sliding clock. *That* is "$c$ is $c$ for everyone."

- Rest clock: mirror meets its circle straight overhead — strike at $t = 2$ (radius $h$).
- Moving clock: the mirror slid, so it meets the **same planted circle** later, off-axis — strike at $t = 2\gamma = {{toFixed(2/sqrt(1 - v^2), 2)}}$.

At that strike: radius $= c\,t$ (hypotenuse), slide $= v\,t$ (base), height $= h$ (leg):

$$(c\,t)^2 = h^2 + (v\,t)^2$$

Pythagoras is *intercepted*, not assumed. The faint red circle is Galileo's dragged wavefront — it would strike at $t = 2$, no dilation, no speed limit.

*Watch the moving photon: it rides exactly **on** its planted wavefront, all flight long.*

steps.title: Gamma readout and reality checks
steps.description: The stretch factor is $\gamma = 1/\sqrt{1-v^2}$. At $v=0.6$ that's $1.25$; as $v \to c$ it rockets toward infinity. This isn't a light-clock quirk — *every* clock, including biology, slows the same way. Muons born 15 km up should decay in ~660 m, yet $\gamma \approx 5\text{–}10$ lets them reach the ground; GPS clocks are corrected ~38 μs/day or your maps drift kilometers off.
steps.prompt: Wrap up with the live gamma readout gamma = 1/sqrt(1-v^2) and the inset curve of gamma(v) with a marker at the current v. Drag v: near v=0.95 gamma blows up past 3. Stress universality: springs, atoms, biology all slow by gamma because a light clock could sit inside any of them. Deliver the two reality checks concretely: muons (15 km up, tau ~2.2us, ~660 m naive range, gamma 5-10 saves them) and GPS (~38 us/day net, SR -7 and GR +45). Tie the boxed gamma in the proof panel to this on-screen readout. Good closing question: 'At what v does gamma = 2?' (v = sqrt(3)/2 ~ 0.87). THE question to expect: 'this only proves LIGHT clocks slow — why would atoms/biology slow identically?' Three-leg answer: (1) Synchronization: if a co-moving atomic clock dilated differently from the light clock, the two would drift apart on board — an internal experiment detecting absolute motion, forbidden by the principle of relativity; in the ship frame both are at rest and agree, and clock coincidences are frame-independent events, so every co-moving clock must share the same gamma. (2) Mechanism: atoms are charges bound by electromagnetic fields signaling at c — every clock is secretly a baroque light clock; all fundamental interactions are Lorentz-invariant, so no process can opt out. (3) Geometry: proper time is worldline length (next scene); clocks are odometers, and road length doesn't depend on the odometer. Evidence: muons dilate by exactly gamma via the WEAK force — no light bouncing involved — plus GPS, Ives-Stilwell, Hafele-Keating: many mechanisms, one gamma, never a split.

info.content:
### Where $\gamma$ comes from
The triangle, written in clock readings ($h = c\,T_0$):

$$(c\,t)^2 = (c\,T_0)^2 + (v\,t)^2 \;\Rightarrow\; t = \gamma\,T_0, \qquad \gamma = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-v^2}}$$

At $v = {{toFixed(v,2)}}$: $\;\gamma = {{toFixed(1/max(sqrt(1-v^2), 0.000001),2)}}$ — not a postulate, just the Pythagorean sum solved for $t$.

Moving clock tick $= \gamma \times$ rest tick. *(As $v \to 1$ the readout is capped near lightspeed; truly $\gamma \to \infty$.)*

### Reality checks
**Muons:** born ~15 km up, would fall only ~660 m before decaying — yet $\gamma \approx 5\text{–}10$ lets them reach the ground.

**GPS:** satellite clocks corrected by **~38 μs/day** (net), or positions drift **kilometers** off within a day.

proof.title: Deriving $\gamma$ from the light clock
proof.prompt: This derivation gets gamma from a single bouncing photon using only Pythagoras and the constancy of c. The lone physical assumption is that light travels at the same speed c in every inertial frame (step 2). Keep the invariant-c thread (#ffaa33) visible through every step and match the on-canvas Pythagoras triangle in step 3. End by boxing gamma and tying it to the live gamma readout in scene step 4.

steps.explanation: In ordinary space the rest clock's photon has no horizontal motion. One tick is simply up-and-back, a distance $2h$ covered at speed $c$.
steps.prompt: Anchor T0 = 2h to the on-canvas stationary photon bouncing straight up and down between the mirrors (height h = 2). Highlight the vertical light path in #ffaa33.

steps.prompt: Emphasize that invariant c is the ONLY assumption. The moving photon cannot go faster than c to compensate for the longer path — that is exactly why time must stretch instead.

leg.label: The same vertical leg — the mirror gap, now written as the rest tick $c\,T_0$

steps.label: Solve for the moving tick
steps.justification: Collect the $\Delta t$ terms and divide — $\gamma$ is born here, as the factor the triangle forces
steps.explanation: The full moving tick is twice this half-tick: $\Delta t_{\text{full}} = \dfrac{2h}{\sqrt{1-v^2}}$. The rest tick was $2h$, so the moving clock's tick is longer by the factor $1/\sqrt{1-v^2}$.
steps.prompt: Show the algebra: move (v*Delta_t)^2 over, factor, divide. The sqrt(1-v^2) denominator emerging (highlight green) is the birth of gamma. Note that doubling gives the full moving tick 2h/sqrt(1-v^2).

scenes.title: The Interval
scenes.description: Rotations preserve $x^2+y^2$; Lorentz boosts preserve $x^2-t^2$. Hyperbolas are the circles of spacetime.
scenes.markdown:
# The Interval

In ordinary geometry, a **rotation** spins a point around the origin without changing its distance from the origin. The quantity that stays fixed is

$$x^2 + y^2 = R^2.$$

Spin a ruler around its end and its tip sweeps a **circle** of radius $R$.

## Minkowski's version

Spacetime has its own invariant. Instead of the distance-squared $x^2+y^2$, the quantity every inertial observer agrees on is the **invariant interval**

$$s^2 = x^2 - t^2 \qquad (c = 1).$$

The minus sign changes everything. Curves of constant $s^2$ are no longer circles — they are **hyperbolas**:

- **Timelike** separations: $t^2 - x^2 = k$ — hyperbolas opening up and down.
- **Spacelike** separations: $x^2 - t^2 = k$ — hyperbolas opening left and right.

Both families share the same **45° light rays** $y = \pm x$ as asymptotes. These hyperbolas are the *circles of Minkowski geometry*: the shapes that stay fixed when observers move.

## Boosts are hyperbolic rotations

A **Lorentz boost** (changing to a frame moving at speed $v$) slides an event along its hyperbola exactly the way a rotation slides a point along a circle. Parametrised by the **rapidity** $\eta$, a boost sends

$$t' = t\cosh\eta - x\sinh\eta, \qquad x' = x\cosh\eta - t\sinh\eta.$$

Because of the hyperbolic identity

$$\cosh^2\eta - \sinh^2\eta = 1,$$

the combination $x^2 - t^2$ is left unchanged. A point starting on the unit spacelike hyperbola at $(\cosh\eta,\ \sinh\eta)$ slides along it forever while $x^2 - t^2$ stays pinned at $1$.

## Proper time and rapidity

Along a moving worldline, a carried clock measures **proper time** $\tau$ with

$$d\tau^2 = dt^2 - dx^2 = dt^2\,(1 - v^2) \;\Rightarrow\; d\tau = \frac{dt}{\gamma},$$

which is exactly the slowdown of the moving light clock. The boost angle is the rapidity $\eta$:

$$v = \tanh\eta, \qquad \gamma = \cosh\eta, \qquad \gamma v = \sinh\eta.$$

Rapidity is the honest angle of spacetime — and, unlike velocity, rapidities **add linearly** when boosts are combined. That linearity is what will make constant acceleration so clean in the next scene.

**Analogy at a glance:** circle $\leftrightarrow$ hyperbola, $\cos/\sin \leftrightarrow \cosh/\sinh$, and $\cos^2+\sin^2=1 \leftrightarrow \cosh^2-\sinh^2=1$.


## "Isn't this all just perspective — a visual illusion?"

No — and the interval is exactly why. Observers *disagree* about $\Delta t$ and $\Delta x$ the way two people disagree about a tilted ruler's shadow: lawfully, and while agreeing perfectly on the ruler itself. Here the "ruler" is the invariant interval $s^2 = \Delta x^2 - \Delta t^2$ — every inertial observer computes the *same* number.

And the effects leave permanent records that no change of viewpoint can undo: a clock's accumulated proper time $\tau$ is the number physically on its face, and everyone agrees what it reads when two worldlines meet. Muons really do reach the ground; GPS clocks really do need their daily correction; a travelling twin really returns younger. Perspectives in relativity are like map projections — Mercator and globe disagree about Greenland, but the distances on the actual ground are what they are. **The interval is the ground.**

*Sources: [Time dilation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_dilation), [Hyperbolic motion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyperbolic_motion_(relativity)).*

## Time is the length of your worldline

Between two fixed events, a clock's total reading is the **Minkowski arc length** of its worldline: $\tau = \int \sqrt{dt^2 - dx^2}$. Because of the minus sign, the triangle inequality flips: a bent (moving) route between the same two events is **shorter** in proper time than the straight one — the traveling twin returns younger by exactly $\sqrt{1-w^2}$. Time dilation, the twin paradox, and (next scene) the slow aging of an accelerating ship are all the same statement: *clocks measure worldline length, and straight worldlines are the longest.*
scenes.prompt: This is Scene 3 of the Rindler lesson ("The Interval"). It introduces the invariant interval s^2 = x^2 - t^2, hyperbolas as the 'circles' of Minkowski geometry, and Lorentz boosts as hyperbolic rotations parametrised by rapidity eta. This is a 2D spacetime diagram: x = space (horizontal), y = ct (vertical), c = 1. Color conventions: worldlines / the boosted point #4da6ff; light rays / asymptotes #ffaa33; the neutral hyperbola family and the Euclidean warm-up circle #aabbcc; axes/labels neutral. Lean HARD on the rotation-vs-boost analogy: circles/cos/sin become hyperbolas/cosh/sinh, and cos^2+sin^2=1 becomes cosh^2-sinh^2=1. The emotional 'aha' is the live readout showing x^2 - t^2 pinned at 1.00 while the boosted point slides along its hyperbola. Recall gamma = 1/sqrt(1-v^2) from the light-clock scene, and note dtau = dt/gamma is the same slowdown. Foreshadow that rapidity adds linearly (unlike velocity), which makes constant proper acceleration clean in the next scene. Predict-before-reveal: in step 3 ask which quantity refuses to change under a boost before advancing. Suggested follow-ups: 'Why hyperbolas and not ellipses?', 'What is rapidity intuitively?', 'How does dtau = dt/gamma connect to the light clock?'.

steps.title: Euclidean warm-up
steps.description: Spin a ruler around its end and its tip traces a circle: $x^2 + y^2$ stays fixed no matter the angle. Rotation preserves distance — hold that thought.
steps.prompt: Ground the intuition in ordinary geometry first. Drag theta and point out that the ruler's tip sweeps a circle of radius R=3, and x^2 + y^2 stays pinned at 9.00 no matter the angle. Emphasize: a rotation is the transformation that preserves x^2 + y^2. Tease that spacetime has its own version of this invariant with a crucial sign change. Suggest the follow-up 'What replaces the circle in spacetime?'.

info.content:
### Rotation preserves distance

$$x^2 + y^2 = {{toFixed(9*cos(theta)^2 + 9*sin(theta)^2, 2)}} = 9.00$$

The tip sweeps a **circle**. The invariant is $x^2+y^2$.

steps.title: Minkowski circles
steps.description: In spacetime the 'circles' are **hyperbolas**. Constant $t^2 - x^2$ opens up and down (timelike); constant $x^2 - t^2$ opens left and right (spacelike). All hug the same 45° light rays.
steps.prompt: Now replace the circle with the hyperbola family. Timelike hyperbolas (t^2 - x^2 = k) open up and down; spacelike hyperbolas (x^2 - t^2 = k) open left and right. Stress that BOTH families share the same 45-degree light rays y = +/- x as asymptotes — those are the light rays from Scene 1. These hyperbolas are the 'circles of Minkowski geometry': the shapes that stay fixed when observers move. The k=1 and k=4 curves show how the family nests. Suggest 'Why do all the hyperbolas approach the same 45-degree lines?'.

info.content:
### The circles of Minkowski geometry

$$t^2 - x^2 = k \;\;(\text{timelike})$$
$$x^2 - t^2 = k \;\;(\text{spacelike})$$

Shown for $k = 1$ and $k = 4$. All share the **45° asymptotes** $y = \pm x$.

steps.title: Predict then reveal: boosting
steps.description: Boost this event — view it from a moving frame — by dragging $\eta$. **Predict first:** which quantity refuses to change? The point glides along the *same* hyperbola; $x^2 - t^2$ stays exactly $1$.
steps.prompt: PREDICT-BEFORE-REVEAL step. First ask the student to predict which quantity stays unchanged as we boost the event (drag eta). Then reveal: the point slides along the very same unit spacelike hyperbola x^2 - t^2 = 1. Its x = cosh(eta) and t = sinh(eta) both change, but x^2 - t^2 = cosh^2(eta) - sinh^2(eta) = 1 always — this is the invariant interval. Point at the live readout pinned at 1.00 as the emotional payoff. Rapidity eta is the boost 'angle': v = tanh(eta). Draw the analogy to the rotating ruler in step 1 — same idea, hyperbola instead of circle. Suggest 'What does eta = 0 correspond to physically?' (the rest frame, the vertex at (1,0)).

info.content:
### Predict, then drag $\eta$

The event sits at $\big(\cosh\eta,\ \sinh\eta\big) = ({{toFixed(cosh(eta),2)}},\ {{toFixed(sinh(eta),2)}})$.

Which quantity refuses to change?

$$x^2 - t^2 = {{toFixed(cosh(eta)^2 - sinh(eta)^2, 2)}} = 1.00$$

Every observer agrees on the **interval**.

steps.title: Proper time and rapidity
steps.description: Along a moving worldline the clock ticks **proper time** $\tau$, with $d\tau = dt/\gamma$ — the same slowdown as the light clock. The boost angle is the rapidity: $v = \tanh\eta$, $\gamma = \cosh\eta$, $\gamma v = \sinh\eta$.
steps.prompt: Tie the boost to the timelike side and to the previous scene. The timelike hyperbola through (0,1) is a worldline; the moving observer measures proper time tau with dtau = dt/gamma — exactly the light-clock slowdown from Scene 2. Show the rapidity dictionary: v = tanh(eta), gamma = cosh(eta), gamma*v = sinh(eta). Point out with the live readout that as eta grows, v = tanh(eta) creeps toward 1 (the light speed limit) but never reaches it, while gamma = cosh(eta) blows up. Crucially, FORESHADOW: rapidity adds linearly when boosts combine (velocities don't), and that is what makes constant proper acceleration clean in the next scene. Suggest 'Why does rapidity add but velocity doesn't?'.

info.content:
### Rapidity dictionary

$$v = \tanh\eta = {{toFixed(tanh(eta),2)}}$$
$$\gamma = \cosh\eta = {{toFixed(cosh(eta),2)}}$$
$$\gamma v = \sinh\eta = {{toFixed(sinh(eta),2)}}$$

Proper time: $d\tau = dt/\gamma$ — the light-clock slowdown. Rapidities **add linearly**.

steps.title: Time is length: two paths, two ages
steps.description: Proper time is the Minkowski length of a worldline — a clock ticks once per unit of $\sqrt{dt^2 - dx^2}$. Count the gold ticks along each route.
steps.prompt: Twin-paradox-in-one-picture step. Two worldlines join the SAME events A (bottom) and B (top): the straight stay-home line and a bent out-and-back route at speed w. Gold dots are clock ticks, one per unit of proper time — literally the distance measured along each worldline with the Minkowski ruler dtau = sqrt(dt^2 - dx^2). Ask the learner to PREDICT first: the bent path looks longer on paper, so which twin ages more? Then reveal: fewer ticks fit on the bent path — tau = 8*sqrt(1-w^2) < 8. Minus sign in the metric reverses the triangle inequality: straight timelike worldlines MAXIMIZE proper time. Connect back to the light clock (moving clocks tick slower) and forward to the accelerating ship, whose curved worldline also ages less than a straight one. Two rider dots now travel A to B in lockstep coordinate time (slider T2, animatable); each gold tick flashes (pulsating size) exactly when its worldline's rider crosses it — the stay-home clock strikes 8 flashes, the traveler fewer. NOTE: the inbound-leg ticks continue the traveler's proper time seamlessly through the turnaround (tick m sits at coordinate time m/sqrt(1-w^2) - 4) — the clock does NOT restart at the turn, so there is no rapid double-strike near the turnaround; the gap across the turn is exactly one unit of tau like every other gap.

info.content:
**Predict first:** the bent route looks *longer* on paper — which twin ages more?

**Proper time = worldline length.** A carried clock ticks once per unit of Minkowski length $d\tau = \sqrt{dt^2 - dx^2}$. Count the gold ticks:

- straight: $\tau = 8.00$
- bent: $\tau = 8\sqrt{1-w^2} = {{toFixed(8*sqrt(1-w^2), 2)}}$

The minus sign in the metric **reverses the triangle inequality**: the detour is *shorter* in spacetime, so the traveler returns *younger*. Straight worldlines maximize aging — that is the twin "paradox", read straight off the geometry.

*Press play on $t$: the two dots travel together from $A$ to $B$, and each tick **flashes** as its twin's clock strikes it. Watch the traveler strike fewer.*

**Now** ($t = {{toFixed(T2, 2)}}$):
stay-home clock $\tau = {{toFixed(T2 + 4, 2)}}$ · traveler clock $\tau = {{toFixed(sqrt(1-w^2)*(T2 + 4), 2)}}$

*Note: the reunion is **fixed by construction** — both twins meet at $B$, 8 Earth-years after $A$. The speed slider changes how far the traveler swings out, not when they return.*

steps.title: The honest twin: constant thrust there and back
steps.description: No teleporting kinks — the ship feels a steady $a$ the whole way: push, flip, brake. Three hyperbolic arcs, same verdict.
steps.prompt: Realistic powered twin. The blue worldline is three hyperbolic arcs of constant FELT acceleration a: thrust outward for the first quarter (t in [-4,-2]), flip and thrust homeward for the whole middle half (t in [-2,2] — one continuous hyperbola through the at-rest turnaround at t=0), flip again and brake into B for the last quarter. Red dots mark the two flips; the ship is momentarily at rest at the turnaround. Proper time is tau = 4*asinh(2a)/a (each quarter contributes asinh(2a)/a), always less than 8 — smooth curves lose time exactly like kinks, because ANY lean toward the light lines shortens the Minkowski length. Gold ticks are the crew's continuous clock; they flash as the riders pass. Cranking a hugs the lightcone tighter: harder thrust, younger crew. Bridge forward: each arc is exactly the hyperbola derived in the next scene.

info.content:
**Same trip, honest engines.** The crew *feels* a steady $a$ the whole way — thrust out for the first quarter, flip and thrust homeward through the middle half (momentarily at rest at the turnaround), flip and brake into $B$. Every arc is a hyperbola.

Felt thrust: $a = {{toFixed(a, 2)}}$ in $c$/yr $\;\approx\; {{toFixed(0.97*a, 2)}}\,g$ — at the default setting the crew simply feels Earth gravity, for eight years.

- straight: $\tau = 8.00$
- powered twin: $\tau = \dfrac{4\,\mathrm{asinh}(2a)}{a} = {{toFixed(4*asinh(2*a)/a, 2)}}$

Smooth curve or sharp kink — **any** lean toward the light lines loses time. Crank $a$: harder thrust hugs the lightcone tighter and ages the crew less. This is the 1g starship's geometry, one scene early.

**Now** ($t = {{toFixed(T3, 2)}}$):
stay-home clock $\tau = {{toFixed(T3 + 4, 2)}}$ · crew clock $\tau = {{toFixed(tw_tau(T3, a), 2)}}$

*Note: the reunion is **fixed by construction** — cranking the thrust widens the swing between the same $A$ and $B$; it does not bring the ship home sooner. (Fixing the destination instead is the trip-numbers chart, two scenes ahead.)*

*Notice how stingy the payoff is: $\tau = 4\,\mathrm{asinh}(2a)/a$ shrinks only **logarithmically** with thrust. At high $a$ the worldline looks glued to $45°$ — but the eye is a terrible $\gamma$-meter: $v=0.95c$ *looks* parallel to the light line yet dilates only $3.2\times$; almost all dilation hides in the last fraction of a degree of slope. Trust the ticks, not the slope — they are the honest ruler.*

### 🚀 Trip dashboard
⏱ ticks — crew: **{{floor(tw_tau(T3, a))}}** · home: **{{floor(T3 + 4)}}**

🏎 velocity: $|v| = {{toFixed(abs(tw_v(T3, a)), 3)}}\,c$ $\;(\gamma = {{toFixed(1/sqrt(1 - tw_v(T3, a)^2), 2)}})$

📍 distance: **{{toFixed(100 * tw_dist(T3, a) / (4*(sqrt(1 + 4*a^2) - 1)/a), 1)}}%** of the round trip

scenes.title: Two Ships Passing: Adding Speeds
scenes.description: Two travelers, adjustable speeds — and the discovery that velocities refuse to add the way Newton promised.
scenes.markdown:
# Two Ships Passing: Adding Speeds

Ship $A$ cruises at $v_A$ and ship $B$ at $v_B$ (signed: positive is rightward), both measured in the stationary frame. The obvious question: **how fast does each see the other move?**

## Newton's answer

Subtract: $w = v_A - v_B$. Head-on at $0.6c$ each, that's $1.2c$ — faster than light, which scene 1 already outlawed.

## Einstein's answer

$$w_{A\,\text{seen from}\,B} = \frac{v_A - v_B}{1 - v_A v_B}$$

Head-on at $0.6c$ each: $1.2/1.36 = 0.882c$. Always below $c$ — even $0.95c$ against $0.95c$ gives only $0.9987c$.

## Why: rapidity is the real adder

Define $\eta = \operatorname{atanh}(v)$ — the hyperbolic angle from The Interval. Boosts compose by **adding rapidities**:

$$\eta_{rel} = \eta_A - \eta_B, \qquad w = \tanh(\eta_A - \eta_B) = \frac{v_A - v_B}{1 - v_A v_B}.$$

Newton's rule is simply the small-angle limit ($\tanh\eta \approx \eta$ when $v \ll c$). Velocities saturate at $c$ because $\tanh$ saturates at $1$ — no matter how much rapidity you stack up.

## And their clocks

Each ship's clock ticks at $\sqrt{1-v^2}$ per stationary-frame second (the light clock). Between the ships themselves, each sees the other slowed by the **same** $\gamma_{rel} = 1/\sqrt{1-w^2}$ — mutual and symmetric. Who is *really* younger has no answer until they reunite, and that requires bending a worldline (the twins).

---
*Sources: [Velocity-addition formula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Velocity-addition_formula) · [Rapidity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rapidity).*
scenes.prompt: Two ships on looping lanes: A (blue rocket) at slider vA, B (green saucer) at slider vB, both signed so learners can set parallel or head-on motion; a gold photon pace-car marks c. Key readouts: B seen from A is (vB-vA)/(1-vA*vB); Newton's naive answer is vB-vA; the bar chart shows Newton's bar piercing the golden c line while Einstein's never reaches it. Rapidity eta = atanh(v) is the quantity that truly adds. Clock counters over each ship tick at their proper rates; dilation between the ships is mutual (same gamma_rel each way) — no asymmetry without a reunion. Encourage extreme-slider experiments: 0.95 against -0.95 still yields under c. Common misconceptions to correct: 'speeds just add', 'one of them is really slower', 'the formula only matters near c'. A third participant O (labeled stationary) stands at x = 0 with a full-rate clock — but stress that 'stationary' is frame convention, not physics: O is at rest in the frame we happen to draw; each ship is equally entitled to call itself at rest and O moving. No experiment distinguishes absolute rest (Michelson-Morley); the closest thing to a cosmically convenient frame is the CMB rest frame, which is special only by contents, not by the laws. Crucial unconfusion when asked: 'c is c for all observers' applies ONLY to light — it does not make sub-light speeds observer-independent. c is the fixed point of the composition law ((1-v)/(1-v) = 1 for any v; infinite rapidity minus finite is still infinite), while every speed below c is a relation that reshuffles between frames — equal speeds cancel to zero relative speed. Sliders now reach ±1 (exactly c). Special-case handling: a ship at |v| = 1 becomes a light ray — label changes, its clock freezes at zero ticks and its tick-flash goes dark (photons accrue no proper time); its rapidity arrow is replaced by an eta = infinity marker. With ONE ship at c the Einstein result lands exactly ON the c tick — the fixed point: (1-v)/(1-v) = 1 for every v. With BOTH at c the composition law returns 0/0, the gap reads infinity minus infinity, and the red banner explains that no rest frame exists at c — the question dissolves rather than answers. Some dashboard numbers may show NaN or Infinity in the both-c case; treat that as the formula honestly reporting indeterminacy, and point to the banner.

steps.title: Two travelers, one stationary frame
steps.description: Set each ship's signed speed — same direction, or head-on. The gold photon is the pace car nobody beats.

Sliders are SIGNED — negative means leftward, so learners can stage parallel cruising or head-on passes. The counters over each ship are their own clocks, ticking at sqrt(1-v^2) per stationary-frame second. Invite play before any formulas. Each participant flashes a light burst on every tick of their OWN clock — O at full rate, ships slower by sqrt(1-v^2); crank a ship's speed and watch its flashes space out. The photon never flashes: zero proper time along a light path — light does not age.

⚡ $B$ is now light itself: **every** observer measures it at exactly $c$ — and it has no clock, no frame, no point of view.
$\tfrac{0}{0}$ — you've asked one photon to measure another. **No rest frame exists at $c$**: the question dissolves. (Einstein's teenage paradox — its resolution was this theory.)

info.content:
**Two travelers, one stationary frame**

All speeds are measured in the *stationary* frame ($c = 1$):

$v_A = {{toFixed(vA, 2)}}$ · $v_B = {{toFixed(vB, 2)}}$

Drag them signed — negative is leftward — and stage a head-on pass or a parallel cruise. The ⏱ counters are each ship's *own* clock. The photon up top never loses a race.

🧍 **O** stands still on the stationary frame — but "still" is pure convention: O is simply *at rest in the frame this diagram is drawn in*. To A, it's O who moves at $-v_A$. No experiment can crown any of them 'really stationary'.

Each traveler **flashes on their own clock tick** — O at the full stationary rate, the ships slower by their $\sqrt{1-v^2}$. The photon never flashes: light accrues **no proper time at all**.

steps.title: Predict: how fast does B rush past A?
steps.description: Head-on at 0.6c each. Newton says 1.2c. Commit to your answer, then advance.
steps.prompt: Predict step — do NOT reveal the composition law yet. With vA = 0.6 and vB = -0.6, Newton's subtraction gives 1.2c, above light speed. Ask the learner: is that what A actually measures? Options: yes 1.2c; exactly c; something below c. Let them commit before advancing.

info.content:
**Predict first**

At $v_A = {{toFixed(vA, 2)}}$ and $v_B = {{toFixed(vB, 2)}}$, Newton subtracts:

$$w_{Newton} = v_B - v_A = {{toFixed(vB - vA, 2)}}$$

…which can exceed the speed nothing exceeds. So: what does $A$ *actually* measure for $B$?
**Faster than $c$? Exactly $c$? Less than $c$?** Commit, then advance.

steps.title: Reveal: how speeds really merge
steps.description: Newton's bar smashes through the light line. Einstein's never touches it.
steps.prompt: The reveal. Two bars race toward the golden c line: gray Newton bar |vB-vA| pierces it, blue Einstein bar |(vB-vA)/(1-vA*vB)| approaches but never reaches. Walk the dashboard: B seen from A, A seen from B (equal magnitudes, opposite signs), Newton's naive number, gamma_rel. Push both sliders to ±0.95 and watch Einstein's bar crush against the line at 0.9987 while Newton claims 1.9. The denominator 1 - vA*vB is the whole magic.

info.content:
**The composition law**

$$w = \frac{v_B - v_A}{1 - v_A v_B}$$

- $B$ seen from $A$: $\;{{toFixed((vB - vA)/(1 - vA*vB), 3)}}\,c$
- $A$ seen from $B$: $\;{{toFixed((vA - vB)/(1 - vA*vB), 3)}}\,c$
- Newton would say: $\;{{toFixed(vB - vA, 2)}}\,c$
- $\gamma_{rel} = {{toFixed(1/sqrt(1 - ((vB - vA)/(1 - vA*vB))^2), 2)}}$

The denominator $1 - v_A v_B$ is the whole trick: it grows exactly fast enough that $|w| < 1$ always. Try $\pm 0.95$: Newton claims $1.9c$; Einstein delivers $0.9987c$.

**And the photon?** Set $v_B = 1$: $\;w = \dfrac{1 - v_A}{1 - v_A} = 1$, *for every* $v_A$. $c$ is the **fixed point** of this law — the one speed no change of observer can move. That's how "$c$ is $c$ for everyone" and "equal speeds cancel to zero" are both true at once.

*Push a slider all the way to $\pm 1$: the scene handles the speed of light as the special case it truly is.*

steps.title: Rapidity: the thing that really adds
steps.description: $\eta = \operatorname{atanh}(v)$ is the **rapidity**: the speed rewritten as a hyperbolic boost *angle*. Angles are **additive** — chaining boosts simply adds the $\eta$'s, with no cap — and $\tanh$ maps the total back below $c$.
steps.prompt: Two rails. Top: rapidity axis with arrows eta_A (blue), eta_B (green), and their plain difference (gold gap) — subtraction happens here, angle-style. Bottom: velocity axis with gold ±c marks and two RESULT arrows: gray Newton (vB - vA, can pierce past +c or -c) and blue Einstein (tanh of the gap, always strictly inside the marks). The story in one picture: convert to angles, subtract like a Newtonian, convert back — that IS the composition law. Newton subtracted on the wrong rail. Walk the proof panel for the two-line tanh identity if asked.

add.text: gap $= \infty - \text{finite} = \infty \;\Rightarrow\; \tanh(\infty) = $ exactly $c$: light outruns every finite rapidity
gap $= \infty - \infty$: **undefined** — there is no frame in which to compare two light rays

info.content:
**Rapidity** $\eta = \operatorname{atanh}(v)$ — velocity re-expressed as the *angle* of the boost (The Interval's hyperbolic rotation). Its superpower: **rapidities are additive**. Compose two boosts and the $\eta$'s just add or subtract, Newton-style, without limit — all the speed-limit magic lives in the $\tanh$ that converts back.

**Two rails**

**Top rail (angles):** 🔵 $\eta_A$ and 🟢 $\eta_B$ are the ships' rapidities, $\eta = \operatorname{atanh}(v)$. The 🟡 gap is their plain difference: $\eta_B - \eta_A = {{toFixed(atanh(vB) - atanh(vA), 2)}}$.

**Bottom rail (speeds):** map that gap back with $\tanh$ —

$$w = \tanh(\eta_B - \eta_A) = {{toFixed((vB - vA)/(1 - vA*vB), 3)}}\,c$$

That's the blue **Einstein** arrow: always inside the gold $\pm c$ marks. The gray **Newton** arrow ($v_B - v_A = {{toFixed(vB - vA, 2)}}\,c$) subtracts speeds directly — and sails past $c$.

Same subtraction, different rail. Newton's only mistake was subtracting on the wrong one.

steps.title: Each other's clocks
steps.description: Both slowed — by the same factor, each in the other's eyes. Symmetric, until someone turns around.
steps.prompt: Close the loop on time. Each ship's counter ticks at its own sqrt(1-v^2) in the STATIONARY frame — but between the ships, dilation is MUTUAL: A judges B slow by gamma_rel and B judges A slow by the SAME gamma_rel. Neither is wrong; they slice spacetime differently. The asymmetry only becomes fact at a reunion, which needs a bent worldline — point back to the twin steps. Also flag the ride-along camera caveat: riding A with the camera is not boosting into A's frame; the stationary frame's clock still runs the animation.

info.content:
**Whose clock is slow?**

In the stationary frame: $A$ ticks at $\sqrt{1-v_A^2} = {{toFixed(sqrt(1 - vA^2), 3)}}$, $B$ at $\sqrt{1-v_B^2} = {{toFixed(sqrt(1 - vB^2), 3)}}$ per stationary-frame second — watch the ⏱ counters.

Between *each other*: $A$ sees $B$ slowed by $\gamma_{rel} = {{toFixed(1/sqrt(1 - ((vB - vA)/(1 - vA*vB))^2), 2)}}$ — and $B$ sees $A$ slowed by **exactly the same factor**. Mutual, symmetric, and both correct.

Who is *really* younger? No fact of the matter — until they meet again, and meeting again means bending a worldline (remember the twins).

*Try the Ride A / Ride B views — but note: the camera follows a ship without boosting; you are still watching in the stationary frame.*

scenes.title: Flooring It Forever
scenes.description: Constant proper acceleration can't be Newton's parabola — nature bends the worldline into the hyperbola $x^2 - t^2 = 1/a^2$.
scenes.markdown:
# Flooring It Forever

You fire a rocket and never let up. What worldline do you trace on the spacetime map ($x$ = space across, $ct$ = time up, $c = 1$)?

## Why Newton breaks

Newton says velocity climbs without bound, $v = a\,t$, so the worldline is the parabola $x = \tfrac{1}{2}a\,t^2$. But at $t = 1/a$ that gives $v = 1$ — the speed of light — and beyond it the worldline is *shallower than $45^\circ$*, meaning it covers more space than time. It leaves the lightcone. **Impossible.** Nothing massive can reach or cross the light line.

## The honest worldline: a hyperbola

Under **constant proper acceleration** $a$ (what an onboard accelerometer reads), rapidity accumulates linearly in proper time, $\eta = a\tau$. Integrating the unit-timelike 4-velocity gives

$$x(\tau) = \tfrac{1}{a}\cosh(a\tau), \qquad ct(\tau) = \tfrac{1}{a}\sinh(a\tau).$$

Since $\cosh^2 - \sinh^2 = 1$, this is the hyperbola

$$x^2 - t^2 = \frac{1}{a^2},$$

with vertex at $(1/a,\,0)$ and asymptote the $45^\circ$ line $x = t$. At low speed it hugs Newton's parabola (slow rockets behave normally), but it curves to stay forever inside the lightcone — kissing the light line without ever crossing it.

## Two different accelerations

- **Coordinate acceleration** $dv/dt$: in Earth's frame $v(t) = \dfrac{t}{\sqrt{1/a^2 + t^2}} \to 1$, so $dv/dt \to 0$. The velocity *fades* toward $c$ but never arrives.
- **Proper acceleration** $a$: the steady, invariant push you feel in your seat — the accelerometer reads a constant $a$ the whole time.

Along the ride, $\gamma = \cosh(a\tau)$ balloons and $v = \tanh(a\tau) < 1$ always. Equal ticks of proper time $\tau$ carry the ship ever further and faster: the engine of the relativistic rocket.

And notice the line the hyperbola forever chases — the asymptote $x = t$. **Remember that line.** In the next scene it becomes a genuine event horizon.

---
*Sources: [Hyperbolic motion (relativity)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyperbolic_motion_(relativity)), [Relativistic rocket FAQ](https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/physics/Relativity/SR/Rocket/rocket.html).*

info.content:

At $t = 1/a = {{toFixed(1/a,2)}}$ the naive velocity reaches $v = 1$.

$$x(\tau)=\tfrac{1}{a}\cosh(a\tau),\ ct(\tau)=\tfrac{1}{a}\sinh(a\tau)$$

info.content:
### Riding by proper time
$$\gamma = \cosh(a\tau) = {{toFixed(cosh(a*tau),2)}}$$
$$v = \tanh(a\tau) = {{toFixed(tanh(a*tau),2)}} < 1$$
Equal ticks of $\tau$ carry the ship ever further and faster. It forever chases $x = t$ — **remember that line.**

**Geometry of the ticks:** the gold dots mark the crew's clock, one per unit of proper time $\tau$ — the distance measured *along* the curved worldline with $d\tau = \sqrt{dt^2 - dx^2}$. Equal ticks stretch ever farther apart in coordinate time as the hyperbola leans toward the $45°$ light line — the continuous version of the twin picture: the more your path tilts, the more height each tick costs.

scenes.title: The Light That Never Catches Up
scenes.description: A photon fired after an accelerating ship rides the asymptote $x = t$ forever — a genuine event horizon made of pure geometry.
scenes.markdown:
# The Light That Never Catches Up

The ship from the last scene accelerates forever along the hyperbola

$$x^2 - t^2 = \frac{1}{a^2}, \qquad x_{\text{ship}}(t) = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{a^2} + t^2}.$$

With $a = 1$ (our natural units) this is simply $x_{\text{ship}}(t) = \sqrt{1 + t^2}$, whose asymptote is the $45^\circ$ light line $x = t$.

## The chase

Fire a photon from the origin straight after the ship. Its worldline **is** the line $x = t$ — exactly the hyperbola's asymptote. As $t \to \infty$,

$$x_{\text{ship}}(t) = \sqrt{1 + t^2} \to t,$$

so the gap $x_{\text{ship}} - x_{\text{photon}} \to 0$: the light creeps closer and closer but **never** catches up. Light, moving at $c$, still can't reach a ship that accelerates forever.

## The catch condition

A rightward photon emitted at the event $(x_e, t_e)$ has worldline $x_{\text{ph}}(t) = x_e + (t - t_e)$. As $t \to \infty$ its lead over the ship tends to the constant $x_e - t_e$. So it catches the ship **iff**

$$x_e - t_e > 0 \iff x_e > t_e,$$

provided the pulse isn't already emitted past the ship. The single $45^\circ$ line $x = t$ cleanly divides *“can reach the ship”* from *“can never reach the ship.”*

## A horizon of pure geometry

The line $x = t$ is a genuine **event horizon** for the accelerating ship. In the ship's instantaneous rest frame it hovers a fixed proper distance behind:

$$d = \frac{c^2}{a}.$$

At a comfortable $1g$ that distance is about $0.97$ light-years. Events beyond the horizon can still *receive* the ship's signals — they just can never *reply*. A one-way membrane, exactly like a black hole's horizon.

## Common misconceptions

- **Not a wall.** Fly through that region and you'd feel nothing locally; it is a purely causal boundary.
- **Light never stops.** It always moves at $c$; “never catching up” is a chase, not a freeze.
- **Not permanent.** Cut the engines and the horizon vanishes; every stranded, redshifted message finally arrives.

_Framing after Greg Egan; see also the relativistic-rocket FAQ (Baez) and Rindler coordinates._
scenes.prompt: This is the emotional peak of the lesson. The ship follows the a=1 hyperbola x = sqrt(1+t^2) (worldline in blue #4da6ff), the chasing photon rides x = t (light/photon in #ffaa33), and the horizon line x = t is vivid red #ff5566. The single most important visual insight: the photon's worldline x = t IS the ship's hyperbola asymptote. Let the word 'never' land. Emphasize the catch condition xe > te as a clean guillotine dividing spacetime: a pulse from (xe, te) catches the ship iff xe - te > 0 (and the emission isn't already ahead of the ship). Name the line x = t the EVENT HORIZON and give the d = c^2/a = 0.97 ly at 1g fact. Explicitly handle the four misconceptions from the research brief: not a physical wall, light never stops, the horizon vanishes if you stop accelerating, and only signals emitted beyond the line are stranded (earlier ones arrive, delayed and redshifted). Suggested follow-ups: 'Why is the asymptote exactly a light ray?', 'What happens if the ship stops accelerating?', 'How is this like a black hole horizon?'. Coordinates: x = space (horizontal), y = ct (vertical), c = 1, z = 0 throughout.

steps.title: Predict: does light catch the ship?
steps.description: The ship blasts away under constant 1g acceleration, tracing its hyperbola. At the origin we fire a light pulse straight after it — the fastest possible chaser. Does the light, given enough time, finally catch the ship? Guess before you advance.
steps.prompt: Set up the chase without revealing the answer. The ship worldline (blue) is the a=1 hyperbola x = cosh(u), t = sinh(u), equivalently x = sqrt(1+t^2). The photon (orange) leaves the origin along x = t. Drive the ship-ride point and the photon with the tau slider — press play to watch them race. Ask the student to commit to a prediction: does the photon ever catch the ship? Do NOT reveal that the answer is 'never' yet.

info.content:
### The chase begins
A light pulse leaves the origin, chasing a ship that accelerates **forever**.

$$x_{\text{ship}} = \sqrt{1 + t^2}, \qquad x_{\text{photon}} = t$$

**Predict:** does the light ever catch up?

- Yes, eventually
- No, never
- It exactly ties

_Commit to a guess, then advance._

steps.title: Reveal: never
steps.description: Never. The photon's worldline x = t is precisely the asymptote the ship's hyperbola chases forever. The ship curves away just fast enough: in its own frame the light creeps closer and closer, the gap shrinking without limit — yet it never closes.
steps.prompt: Reveal the answer: NEVER. Make the key point vivid — the photon line x = t (now drawn as a full red-orange asymptote) IS the hyperbola's asymptote. Show the live gap readout x_ship - x_photon shrinking toward zero as tau grows but never reaching it. Emphasize: same speed c, but the ship keeps bending away. This shrinking-but-never-closing gap is the heart of the whole scene.

info.content:
### Never.
The photon's worldline $x = t$ is **exactly** the hyperbola's asymptote.

$$x_{\text{ship}} - x_{\text{photon}} = \sqrt{1 + t^2} - t \;\longrightarrow\; 0$$

Live gap at $\tau$: $\;{{toFixed(cosh(tau) - sinh(tau), 3)}}$

The gap shrinks without limit but **never** reaches zero. Same speed $c$, yet the ship bends away just fast enough.

steps.title: Move the emission event
steps.description: Now move where the pulse is fired. Send it from an event in front of the line x = t (xe > te) and it catches the ship. Send it from on or behind that line (xe <= te) and it is doomed — forever chasing, never arriving. One 45-degree line divides 'can reach the ship' from 'can never reach the ship.'
steps.prompt: Let the student place the emission event (xe, te) with the two sliders. The photon worldline is x = xe + (t - te), drawn from the event up-right at 45 degrees (orange). The verdict updates live: catches iff xe - te > 0 AND the emission is not already ahead of the ship (xe < sqrt(1 + te^2)). When it catches, a marker appears at the catch event and the info reads CATCHES with catch time t* = (1 - (xe-te)^2)/(2(xe-te)); otherwise NEVER. Keep the horizon line x = t visible as the divider. Encourage sweeping xe across te to feel the clean threshold.

add.text: $\text{Emitted ahead of the ship — it leads forever.}$

info.content:
### Where you fire matters
Emission event: $(x_e, t_e) = ({{toFixed(xe, 2)}},\ {{toFixed(te, 2)}})$

Lead over the ship at large $t$: $\;x_e - t_e = {{toFixed(xe - te, 2)}}$

- $x_e - t_e > 0$ → **catches the ship** at $t^{*} = \dfrac{1-(x_e-t_e)^2}{2(x_e-t_e)}$
- $x_e - t_e \le 0$ → **never catches** — it chases forever

The line $x = t$ is the clean divider — watch the verdict on the canvas.

steps.title: Name it: the event horizon
steps.description: This line deserves its name: an EVENT HORIZON, built from nothing but geometry. In the ship's instantaneous frame it hovers a fixed proper distance behind: d = c^2/a. Accelerate at 1g and a horizon hangs about 0.97 light-years behind you, riding along at a constant distance.
steps.prompt: Name the line x = t the EVENT HORIZON, drawn boldly in red #ff5566. Give the horizon proper-distance fact d = c^2/a = 1/a in natural units, which at 1g is about 0.97 light-years. Show the fixed proper-distance gap behind the ship at a sample proper time (the perpendicular offset of magnitude 1 = 1/a from the ship back toward the horizon along the instantaneous rest line). Stress it is a one-way membrane: events beyond can receive the ship's signals but can never reply — just like a black hole horizon.

info.content:
### An event horizon from pure geometry
The line $x = t$ is a genuine **event horizon** for the accelerating ship.

In the ship's instantaneous rest frame it sits a **fixed** proper distance behind:

$$d = \frac{c^2}{a}.$$

At $1g$: $\;d \approx 0.97$ light-years — a horizon hanging one light-year behind you, no matter how long you burn.

**One-way membrane:** events beyond it can still *receive* your signals, but can never *reply* — just like a black hole's horizon.

*($d$ is the proper — Minkowski — distance along the ship's now-line, not the ruler length of the little segment drawn on screen.)*

steps.title: Bust the myths
steps.description: Three corrections. The horizon is not a wall — fly through and feel nothing. Light never stops or slows; it always moves at c. And cut your engines and the horizon simply vanishes — every stranded, redshifted message finally arrives. The horizon exists only as long as you keep accelerating.
steps.prompt: Close the scene by explicitly busting the misconceptions from the research brief. (1) Not a physical wall: nothing is there locally; it is a purely causal boundary. (2) Light never stops or slows; it always moves at c — 'never catching up' is a chase, not a freeze. (3) The horizon is not permanent: stop accelerating and it vanishes, and all the delayed, redshifted, previously stranded messages finally arrive. Also clarify the subtle point that only signals emitted at events beyond the line x = t are stranded; earlier signals always arrive. Keep the red horizon line as the summarizing image.

add.text: 1.  Not a wall — nothing is there locally
2.  Light never stops — always moves at $c$

info.content:
### Myth 1 — “it’s a wall”
**No.** Fly through that region and you'd feel nothing. The horizon is a purely *causal* boundary, not a physical object. Locally, spacetime there is perfectly ordinary flat space.

### Myth 2 — “light stops at the horizon”
**No.** Light always moves at $c$. The “never catching up” is a *chase that never closes*, not a freeze. Nothing about the light slows down.

### Myth 3 — “the horizon is permanent”
**No.** Cut the engines and the horizon simply **vanishes**. Every message that was stranded beyond it — delayed and redshifted — finally arrives. Only signals emitted at events *beyond* $x = t$ are ever stranded; earlier ones always reach you.

The horizon exists **only while you keep accelerating.**

scenes.title: The Accelerated Observer's Map
scenes.description: The Rindler wedge: an accelerating traveler's own graph paper, gravitational time dilation in flat spacetime, and real 1g interstellar trip numbers.
scenes.markdown:
# The Accelerated Observer's Map

Every accelerating traveler deserves their own graph paper. The **Rindler coordinates** $(\xi, \eta)$ give the uniformly accelerating observer a natural map of the spacetime they can reach.

$$X = \xi\cosh\eta, \qquad T = \xi\sinh\eta$$

- **Curves of constant $\xi$** are hyperbolas $X^2 - T^2 = \xi^2$ — each one the worldline of an observer with proper acceleration $a = 1/\xi$.
- **Lines of constant $\eta$** fan out through the origin with slope $\tanh\eta$ — the shared *moments* of Rindler time.

The two $45^\circ$ lines $x = \pm t$ are **horizons**. They cut spacetime into four wedges: **I** (right — where the observer lives, accessible), **II** (future, beyond the horizon), **III** (left), **IV** (past).

## Gravity out of pure acceleration

Smaller $\xi$ means larger acceleration $a = 1/\xi$ — like hovering ever closer to a black hole. The Rindler metric,

$$ds^2 = -\xi^2\, d\eta^2 + d\xi^2,$$

carries a position-dependent $-\xi^2$ in front of $d\eta^2$: **clocks at smaller $\xi$ tick slower.** That is *gravitational time dilation* — appearing here in perfectly flat spacetime. By the **equivalence principle**, constant acceleration is locally indistinguishable from gravity, so the back of an accelerating rocket behaves like the bottom of a gravity well.

## The payoff: 1g and the galaxy

Accelerate at a steady $1g$ and the numbers become jaw-dropping, because your ship clock counts **proper time** $\tau$, not Earth time. With $c/a \approx 0.97\ \text{yr}$ and $c^2/a \approx 0.97\ \text{ly}$:

$$t_{\text{Earth}} = \tfrac{c}{a}\sinh\!\big(\tfrac{a\tau}{c}\big), \qquad d = \tfrac{c^2}{a}\Big(\cosh\!\big(\tfrac{a\tau}{c}\big) - 1\Big)$$

| Destination | Distance | Ship time (flyby) | Earth time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proxima Centauri | 4.3 ly | ~2.4 y | ~5.1 y |
| Vega | 27 ly | ~4.1 y | ~28 y |
| Galactic center | 30,000 ly | ~11 y | ~30,000 y |
| Andromeda | 2.54 M ly | **~15.5 y** | ~2.54 M y |

These are continuous-thrust **flyby** times — you streak past the destination at nearly $c$, which is what the chart's curve $d = \cosh(a\tau) - 1$ describes. Prefer to *arrive at rest*? Flip the ship and brake for the second half: ship time roughly doubles (Proxima ~3.6 y, Vega ~6.6 y, galactic center ~20 y, Andromeda **~28 y**).

## Outlook

Near a black-hole horizon spacetime looks locally Rindler (equivalence principle). And an accelerating observer even sees the vacuum glow faintly warm — the **Unruh effect**, $T = \dfrac{\hbar a}{2\pi c k_B} \approx 4\times10^{-21}\,\text{K}$ at $1g$ — the flat-spacetime cousin of Hawking radiation.

**Sources:** [Rindler coordinates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rindler_coordinates) · [Relativistic rocket FAQ](https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/physics/Relativity/SR/Rocket/rocket.html) · [Egan, Rindler Horizon](https://www.gregegan.net/SCIENCE/Rindler/RindlerHorizon.html) · [Unruh effect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unruh_effect)
scenes.prompt:
This is the final teaching scene of the lesson (a hand-authored sandbox follows). It gives the accelerating observer their own coordinate system (the Rindler wedge) and delivers the emotional payoff: real 1g interstellar trip numbers.

Axes: x = space (horizontal), y = ct (vertical), z = 0, c = 1. Color conventions (reuse across the lesson): Rindler grid = green #66cc88 (constant-xi hyperbolas AND constant-eta lines); ship/worldlines = blue #4da6ff; horizon lines x = +/- t = red #ff5566; light rays = orange #ffaa33; neutral text/axes = #aabbcc.

Key ideas to emphasize: (1) constant-xi hyperbolas are accelerating observers with a = 1/xi; constant-eta lines through the origin are moments of Rindler time. (2) Four wedges bounded by the horizons x = +/- t; the observer lives entirely in wedge I. (3) The step-3 'aha': the back clock (smaller xi, larger a, nearer the horizon) ticks slower -- gravitational time dilation appearing in FLAT spacetime, via the equivalence principle. Reference the Rindler metric ds^2 = -xi^2 deta^2 + dxi^2: the -xi^2 coefficient is the source. (4) The trip numbers are the crowd-pleaser -- always contrast SHIP time vs EARTH time, and make Andromeda-in-~28-ship-years land hard. Note the chart in step 4 is log-log (each gridline is a factor of ten) because the distances span from ~4 ly to millions of ly.

Misconceptions to correct if asked: the Rindler horizon is observer-dependent and vanishes if you stop accelerating (NOT identical to a black hole, but its flat-spacetime cousin); smaller xi = fiercer acceleration, not slower motion. Suggested follow-ups: 'Why does the back clock run slow?', 'What is the Unruh effect?', 'How can Andromeda be 28 years away?'. Bridge explicitly to the finale sandbox scene that follows this one, where the student rides to the horizon themselves.

info.content:
### Rindler coordinates
$$X = \xi\cosh\eta,\quad T = \xi\sinh\eta$$
Hyperbola $\xi$ = const $\Rightarrow$ an accelerating observer.
Radial line $\eta$ = const $\Rightarrow$ a moment of Rindler time.

Drag $\eta$: the bright line is the moment $\eta = {{toFixed(eta_r, 2)}}$, slope $\tanh\eta = {{toFixed(tanh(eta_r), 2)}}$.

steps.title: Four wedges, $a = 1/\xi$
steps.description: The horizon lines cut spacetime into four wedges. Our observer lives in **wedge I** (right), 'at rest' at constant $\xi$ — yet accelerating at $a = 1/\xi$. Smaller $\xi$, closer to the horizon, means fiercer acceleration: exactly like hovering nearer a black hole.
steps.prompt: Shade and name the four wedges: I (right, accessible), II (future, beyond horizon), III (left), IV (past). Stress that 'sitting still' at constant xi in this accelerated map still means proper acceleration a = 1/xi. Draw the black-hole-hovering analogy: smaller xi = fiercer acceleration = deeper in the effective gravity well. Wedges II and IV are causally cut off from wedge I by the horizons.

info.content:
### $a = \dfrac{1}{\xi}$
A constant-$\xi$ observer is **at rest** in Rindler coordinates but accelerates at
$$a = \frac{1}{\xi}.$$
**Smaller $\xi$** (nearer the horizon) $\Rightarrow$ **larger $a$** — like hovering ever closer to a black hole. Wedge I is all this observer can ever reach.

steps.title: Predict/reveal: clocks along the rocket
steps.description: Two clocks on one long accelerating rocket: **back** at $\xi = 0.7$ (high $a$) and **front** at $\xi = 1.5$ (low $a$). **Predict** which runs slower — then advance. **Reveal:** the back clock, nearer the horizon, ticks slower. That is *gravitational time dilation*, straight from the equivalence principle — in flat spacetime.
steps.prompt: This is the conceptual peak of the scene. First pose the prediction (which clock runs slower, back or front?), then reveal: the BACK clock at smaller xi ticks slower. Explain via the Rindler metric ds^2 = -xi^2 deta^2 + dxi^2 -- the proper-time rate is proportional to xi, so smaller xi = slower clock. Tie it to the equivalence principle: constant acceleration mimics gravity, so 'lower/back' in the rocket = 'deeper in a gravity well'. Marvel that gravitational time dilation appears here with zero curvature.

info.content:
### Predict
Two clocks on one rigid accelerating rocket:
- **back** $\xi = 0.7$ (acceleration $a = {{toFixed(1/0.7, 2)}}$)
- **front** $\xi = 1.5$ (acceleration $a = {{toFixed(1/1.5, 2)}}$)

Which clock runs **slower**? Commit, then read on.

### Reveal
The **back** clock (smaller $\xi$). From the Rindler metric
$$ds^2 = -\xi^2\,d\eta^2 + d\xi^2,$$
proper-time rate $\propto \xi$. This is **gravitational time dilation** in flat spacetime — the equivalence principle at work.

steps.title: Trip numbers at $1g$
steps.description: The payoff. At a steady $1g$, distance covered is $d = \cosh(a\tau) - 1$ and Earth time is $t = \sinh(a\tau)$ (units of $0.97$ ly and $0.97$ yr). Drag $\tau$ along the curve: Proxima flyby in ~2.4 ship-years, the galactic center in ~11, and **Andromeda in ~15.5 years of your life** — while Earth waits 2.5 million. (Braking to arrive at rest roughly doubles these: Andromeda ~28 y.) (Log–log axes: each gridline is $\times 10$.)
steps.prompt: This is the crowd-pleaser. The chart is LOG-LOG (each gridline = factor of 10) because distances span 4 ly to millions of ly. The horizontal axis is distance covered d (ly), the vertical axis is Earth-elapsed time (yr); the curve hugs the diagonal because at high speed the ship covers almost one light-year per Earth year. The moving blue point rides the curve as tau_trip increases. Hammer the ship-time vs Earth-time contrast: the destination labels give SHIP proper time for a continuous-thrust FLYBY (~2.4 y Proxima ... ~15.5 y Andromeda), consistent with the plotted curve d = cosh(a*tau) - 1; mention that flipping and braking to arrive AT REST roughly doubles ship time (Andromeda ~28 y, the famous figure). Earth time is the vertical coordinate (up to millions of years). Explain WHY: proper time is compressed by gamma = cosh(a*tau).

info.content:
### $1g$ journey
$$d = \cosh(a\tau) - 1,\qquad t = \sinh(a\tau)$$
(units: $c^2/a \approx 0.97$ ly, $c/a \approx 0.97$ yr)

**Ship time** $\tau = {{toFixed(tau_trip, 2)}}$
- distance $d \approx {{toFixed(0.97*(cosh(tau_trip) - 1), 2)}}$ ly
- Earth time $t \approx {{toFixed(0.97*sinh(tau_trip), 2)}}$ yr
- $\gamma = \cosh(a\tau) \approx {{toFixed(cosh(tau_trip), 2)}}$

Ship time barely moves while Earth time explodes — that is the compression of proper time by $\gamma$.

steps.title: Wrap-up and bridge
steps.description: You built the whole picture: read a spacetime map, derived time dilation from a bouncing photon, found the invariant interval, bent a worldline into a hyperbola, watched a horizon of pure geometry appear behind the ship, and mapped it in the accelerated observer's own coordinates. Next — the sandbox: ride to the horizon yourself.
steps.prompt: Closing beat. Recap the arc of the whole lesson in one breath (spacetime diagrams -> light-clock gamma -> invariant interval -> hyperbolic worldline -> Rindler horizon -> Rindler map). Then deliver the outlook: near a black hole spacetime looks locally Rindler (equivalence principle), and an accelerating observer sees the vacuum glow faintly warm -- the Unruh effect, T = hbar*a/(2*pi*c*kB) ~ 4e-21 K at 1g, Hawking radiation's flat-spacetime cousin. Explicitly hand off to the finale sandbox scene that follows, where the student rides to the horizon themselves. Keep the Rindler grid and horizon on screen as the summarizing image.
